title: 1908 Summer Olympics

infobox.key: [Opened by](/wiki/List_of_people_who_have_opened_the_Olympic_Games "List of people who have opened the Olympic Games")
infobox.value: [King](/wiki/Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom "Monarchy of the United Kingdom") [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII")[[1]](#cite_note-1)

(officially the **Games of the IV Olympiad** and also known as **London 1908**) were an [international](/wiki/International_sport "International sport") [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, from 27 April to 31 October 1908.[[2]](#cite_note-athletes_number-2) The 1908 Games were originally scheduled to be held in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). On 28 January 1906, It was announced that the 1908 Games were relocated on financial grounds following the eruption of [Mount Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius") in 1906. Rome eventually hosted the Games in [1960](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The **1908 Summer Olympics** (officially the **Games of the IV Olympiad** and also known as **London 1908**) were an [international](/wiki/International_sport "International sport") [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") held in [London](/wiki/London "London"), England, from 27 April to 31 October 1908.[[2]](#cite_note-athletes_number-2) The 1908 Games were originally scheduled to be held in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"). On 28 January 1906, It was announced that the 1908 Games were relocated on financial grounds following the eruption of [Mount Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius") in 1906. Rome eventually hosted the Games in [1960](/wiki/1960_Summer_Olympics "1960 Summer Olympics").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

These were the fourth chronological modern [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") in keeping with the now-accepted four-year cycle as opposed to the alternate four-year cycle of the proposed [Intercalated Games](/wiki/Intercalated_Games "Intercalated Games"). The [IOC president](/wiki/President_of_the_International_Olympic_Committee "President of the International Olympic Committee") for these Games was [Baron Pierre de Coubertin](/wiki/Pierre_de_Coubertin "Pierre de Coubertin").

Lasting a total of 187 days (six months and four days), these were the longest Games in modern Olympics history.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1908_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

There were four bids for the 1908 Summer Olympics. [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") was selected ahead of [London](/wiki/London "London"), [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") and [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"). The selection was made at the 6th IOC Session in London in 1904.[[5]](#cite_note-votes-5)

[Italian](/wiki/Italy "Italy") authorities were preparing to stage the games when [Mount Vesuvius](/wiki/Mount_Vesuvius "Mount Vesuvius") erupted on 7 April 1906, devastating the city of [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"). Funds were diverted to the reconstruction of Naples, so a new host country was required. During the Intercalated Games of Athens, still in April 1906, Coubertin approached Lord Desborough about alternatively hosting in London. Accepting the offer and with a mere 18 months in which to prepare, London was selected for the first time to hold the Games, which were held at [White City](/wiki/White_City,_London "White City, London") alongside the [Franco-British Exhibition](/wiki/Franco-British_Exhibition "Franco-British Exhibition"), at the time the more noteworthy event.[[6]](#cite_note-GT2025-6)

The [White City Stadium](/wiki/White_City_Stadium "White City Stadium"), built in short time for the Games, held 68,000 people, with full crowds turning up to watch the events. The stadium track was three laps to the mile (536.448 metres per lap), as the current standard of 400 metres did not exist until 1962, with a pool (for swimming and diving events) and platforms (for wrestling and gymnastics) in the centre field.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The distance from the start of the [marathon](/wiki/Marathon_(sport) "Marathon (sport)") to the finish at the stadium was established at these Games: the original distance of 25 miles was changed to 26 miles so the marathon could start at [Windsor Castle](/wiki/Windsor_Castle "Windsor Castle") and then changed again at the request of [Princess Mary](/wiki/Mary,_Princess_Royal_and_Countess_of_Harewood "Mary, Princess Royal and Countess of Harewood") so the start would be beneath the windows of the Royal Nursery.[[8]](#cite_note-The_modern_Olympics:_an_overview-8) To ensure that the race would finish in front of the King, the finish line was moved by British officials who "felt compelled to restore the importance of the monarchy." As a result of these changes, the marathon covered a distance of 26 miles and 385 yards (42.195 km), which became the standard length starting with the [1924 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1924_Summer_Olympics "1924 Summer Olympics").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## The Games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1908_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: The Games")]

There were controversies at the games. On the opening day, following the practice introduced at the [Intercalated Games of 1906](/wiki/1906_Summer_Olympics "1906 Summer Olympics"), teams paraded behind [national flags](/wiki/National_flag "National flag"). However, the arrangement caused complications:

* Since [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") was part of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), members of the Finnish team were expected to march under the [Russian](/wiki/Russia "Russia") rather than [Finnish flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Finland "Flag of Finland"), so many chose to march without a flag at all.
* The [Swedish](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") flag had not been displayed above the stadium, so the members of the Swedish team decided not to take part in the ceremony.
* The United States' flag bearer, [Ralph Rose](/wiki/Ralph_Rose "Ralph Rose"), refused to [dip](/wiki/Flag_dipping "Flag dipping") the flag to [King-Emperor Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") in the royal box. His fellow athlete [Martin Sheridan](/wiki/Martin_Sheridan "Martin Sheridan") allegedly declared that "this flag dips to no earthly King." The quote is held as an example of Irish and American defiance of the British monarchy, though its [historicity](/wiki/Historicity "Historicity") is disputed.[[10]](#cite_note-la84foundation.org-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1908_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Events")]

The 1908 Olympics also prompted establishment of standard rules for sports, and selection of judges from different countries rather than just the host. One reason was the 400 metre race, in which a US runner, [John Carpenter](/wiki/John_Carpenter_(athlete) "John Carpenter (athlete)"), was accused by the British officials of interfering with a British runner. Part of the problem was the different definition of interference under British and international rules (the events were held under British rules by the decision of the Organising Committee). The officials decided to disqualify Carpenter and ordered a second final race without him. British [Halswelle](/wiki/Wyndham_Halswelle "Wyndham Halswelle") was to face the other two finalists. These athletes, [William Robbins](/wiki/William_Robbins_(athlete) "William Robbins (athlete)") and [John Taylor](/wiki/John_Taylor_(relay_runner) "John Taylor (relay runner)"), were both Americans and decided not to participate in the repeat of the final to protest against the judges' decision. Halswelle was thus the only medallist in the 400 metres.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Dorando_Pietri_1908.jpg/250px-Dorando_Pietri_1908.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dorando_Pietri_1908.jpg)

Dorando Pietri finishes the marathon.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/1908_Olympics_wax_duel_field.png/250px-1908_Olympics_wax_duel_field.png)](/wiki/File:1908_Olympics_wax_duel_field.png)

Original caption: "One of the most curious contests at the Olympic Games is the duelling with wax bullets. The combatants are as elaborately protected as a German student duellist, and even the revolver has a large hand-guard. The helmet has a plate-glass window."

The most famous incident of the games came at the end of the marathon. [Dorando Pietri](/wiki/Dorando_Pietri "Dorando Pietri"), Italy, began his race at a rather slow pace, but in the second half of the course began a powerful surge moving him into second position by the 32 km (20 mi) mark, 4 minutes behind [South African](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa") [Charles Hefferon](/wiki/Charles_Hefferon "Charles Hefferon"). When he knew that Hefferon was in crisis, Pietri further increased his pace, overtaking him at the 39 km (24 mi) mark.

The effort took its toll and with only two kilometres to go, Pietri began to feel the effects of extreme fatigue and [dehydration](/wiki/Dehydration "Dehydration"). When he entered the stadium, he took the wrong path and when umpires redirected him, he fell down for the first time. He got up with their help, in front of 75,000 spectators.

He fell four more times, and each time the umpires helped him up. In the end, though totally exhausted, he managed to finish the race in first place. Of his total time of 2h 54min 46s, ten minutes were needed for that last 340 metres. Second was American [Johnny Hayes](/wiki/Johnny_Hayes "Johnny Hayes"). The American team immediately lodged a complaint against the help Pietri received from the umpires. The complaint was accepted and Pietri was disqualified and removed from the final standings of the race. Since he had not been responsible for his disqualification, [Queen Alexandra](/wiki/Alexandra_of_Denmark "Alexandra of Denmark") awarded him a gilded silver cup the next day.

These Games were the first to include winter events, as had originally been proposed for the Games. There were four [figure skating](/wiki/Figure_skating_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Figure skating at the 1908 Summer Olympics") events, although held on 28 and 29 October, months after most of the other events.

[Oscar Swahn](/wiki/Oscar_Swahn "Oscar Swahn") from [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), who won the gold medal for [running deer shooting](/wiki/100_meter_running_deer "100 meter running deer"), became the oldest Olympic champion of all time, and set another age record by being 72 years and 279 days old during his triumph at the [1920 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics") in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). One of the more unusual [shooting events in 1908](/wiki/Shooting_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Shooting at the 1908 Summer Olympics") was [Olympic dueling](/wiki/Olympic_dueling "Olympic dueling"). The discipline, which was an associate event (that is, not official), was performed by facing opponents wearing protective clothing and masks and firing wax bullets.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

American [John Taylor](/wiki/John_Taylor_(relay_runner) "John Taylor (relay runner)") was a member of the winning medley relay team, making him the first African-American athlete to win an Olympic gold medal.[[13]](#cite_note-13) Times for the winning team were United States (3:29.4): William Hamilton, 200 metres (22.0); Nathaniel Cartmell, 200 metres (22.2); John Taylor, 400 metres (49.8); and [Melvin Sheppard](/wiki/Melvin_Sheppard "Melvin Sheppard"), 800 metres (1:55.4).[[14]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-14) Less than five months after returning from the Olympic Games in London, Taylor died of [typhoid fever](/wiki/Typhoid_fever "Typhoid fever") on 2 December 1908 at the age of 26.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

The budget of the organising committee showed a cost of [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")15,000; over one-third was labelled "entertainment expense". Donations were the major source of revenue; only 28% of income derived from ticket sales. Total receipts of £21,378 resulted in organisers claiming a profit. Construction of the White City Stadium, which cost the government about £60,000, was not counted.[[16]](#cite_note-Zarnowski-16)

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1908_Summer_Olympics&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sports")]

Twenty-two sports, representing 110 events in 25 sporting disciplines, were contested. A golf tournament had also been planned but it was cancelled a few days before it was scheduled to start.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Swimming, diving, and water polo are considered three disciplines of the same sport, *aquatics*. At the time, tug-of-war was part of athletics and the two different [football](/wiki/Football "Football") codes ([association](/wiki/Football_(soccer) "Football (soccer)") and [rugby (union)](/wiki/Rugby_football "Rugby football")) were listed together. The International Olympic Committee now considers tug-of-war a separate sport as well as referring to association football as simply "football" and to rugby union as "rugby."[[18]](#cite_note-OldSports-18) In one of seven cycling events (cycling sprint) no medals were awarded. The sailing program was open for a total of five sailing classes, but actually only four sailing events were contested. The number of events in each discipline is noted in parentheses.

Thirteen sports venues were used for the 1908 Summer Olympics. The first winter sports took place at Prince's Skating Club in Knightsbridge. White City Stadium served as a precursor to modern stadiums.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The figure skating events were not held at the [next Olympics](/wiki/1912_Summer_Olympics "1912 Summer Olympics") in [Stockholm](/wiki/Stockholm "Stockholm"), but returned for the [1920 Games in Antwerp](/wiki/1920_Summer_Olympics "1920 Summer Olympics"), heralding the [first Winter Olympics](/wiki/1924_Winter_Olympics "1924 Winter Olympics") that took place in [Chamonix](/wiki/Chamonix "Chamonix") in 1924. White City was the main venue for the [1934 British Empire Games](/wiki/1934_British_Empire_Games "1934 British Empire Games") (known as the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") since [1978](/wiki/1978_Commonwealth_Games "1978 Commonwealth Games")) and, before its demolition in 1985, also served as a venue for the [1966 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1966_FIFA_World_Cup "1966 FIFA World Cup"). The All England Lawn Tennis Club continues to host [the Wimbledon championships](/wiki/The_Championships,_Wimbledon "The Championships, Wimbledon") and is the only venue of the 1908 Games that was used for the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Bisley and Henley served as [venues in the 1948 Games](/wiki/1948_Summer_Olympics#Venues "1948 Summer Olympics") when the Olympics returned to London forty years later.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Number of participating athletes per country

The 1908 Games featured athletes representing 22 [National Olympic Committees](/wiki/National_Olympic_Committee "National Olympic Committee"). [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") (as part of the team from [Australasia](/wiki/Australasia "Australasia")) made their first appearance at the Olympic Games. The fact that the United Kingdom competed as a single team was upsetting to some Irish competitors, who felt that Ireland should compete on its own, despite being part of the UK at the time. They relied on the precedent set by [Grand Duchy of Finland](/wiki/Finland_at_the_1908_Summer_Olympics "Finland at the 1908 Summer Olympics"), which while being a part of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire"), competed in London as a separate country.[[35]](#cite_note-officialreport-35) Fearing an Irish boycott, the authorities changed the name of the team to Great Britain/Ireland, and in two sports, field hockey and polo, Ireland participated as a separate country, winning silver medals in both, although their medals counted towards the UK's tally.[[36]](#cite_note-36) Irish athletes in the United States were not affected by this controversy, and many Irish immigrants to the United States competed for the [U.S. Olympic team](/wiki/United_States_at_the_Olympics "United States at the Olympics") as members of the [Irish American Athletic Club](/wiki/Irish_American_Athletic_Club "Irish American Athletic Club"). Members of the Irish American Athletic Club won ten of the U.S. Olympic team's total 23 gold medals, or as many as the nations of [France](/wiki/France "France"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany") and [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") combined.

Britain had more than a quarter of competitors in their team, with 676, compared to 112 on the American team which placed second in the medal standings.
